headline: HEAVY SLOP WARNING: A SLOW-MOVING BROETRY SYSTEM STALLS OVER THE LINKEDIN PLATEAU, DROPPING SINGLE-SENTENCE PARAGRAPHS AND FABRICATED TAXI-DRIVER PARABLES ACROSS THE PROFESSIONAL LOWLANDS
advisory: A dense front of machine-generated inspirational posts has settled over the LinkedIn Plateau and shows no sign of dispersing before Monday. Residents are advised that any story beginning with a stranger who imparts sudden business wisdom before vanishing should be treated as unverified. The Service reminds professionals that no one has ever actually been fired, rehired, and promoted to VP within the span of a 200-word post. Scroll with caution.

regional_outlook.conditions: Total broetry saturation with line breaks after every clause. Expect heavy accumulation of the phrase 'Let that sink in,' with visibility reduced to a single sentence per screen.
Persistent haze as synthetic 'thought leadership' articles are indexed faster than they can be read. Fog expected to thicken near the second page, which no one will reach.
Scattered AI-listing drizzle over the craft sector. Handmade banners flap above inventories that were generated, not made.
Warm, stagnant conditions favorable to reposted motivational graphics. Elevated risk of the same quote misattributed to four different historical figures.
Intermittent showers of near-identical productivity apps, each promising to fix the exact overwhelm the previous nine caused.

specimen.status: Abundant; peaks on Sunday evenings and quarterly-report weeks
specimen.habitat: The LinkedIn Plateau, especially threads about 'lessons in leadership'
specimen.field_marks: A short narrative in which the poster encounters a humble stranger — a taxi driver, a janitor, an elderly barista — who delivers a polished corporate insight and then departs, never to be seen again. Paragraphs are one sentence long. The final line is always its own paragraph.
specimen.call: The cab driver looked at me. Then he said something I'll never forget. 'Culture eats strategy for breakfast.' I sat in silence. We're hiring.
specimen.confusion_species: Can be mistaken for a genuine anecdote. Distinguish by the vanishing wise stranger, the improbably brand-safe dialogue, and the recruiting pitch bolted to the end. A real memory usually resists being a case study.

spotter_tip: If the story's minor character speaks in complete quarterly-strategy sentences and then disappears without a name, you are not reading a memory — you are reading a prompt that got out.
tomorrow: Broetry lingers into Monday morning, with a rising front of 'Happy Monday, let's crush it' posts moving in by 9 a.m. local time.
